content.full_text:
Farn Polrrlcal PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ o Fax (916) 322-0886
Decernber 6,2AO4
Bill Schneiderman
Mi-Wuk/Sugar Pine
Fire Protection District
Post Office Box 530
Mi-Wuk Village, California 95346-053A
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-228
CONCLUSIONS
I ' Yes. As we understand the facts, the board members do not have a conflict
of interest under the Act in voting on a policy under which the district will
purchase supplies from local businesses.
' Go'ernment Code sections 81000 - gl)l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections lgl09-
18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not confei the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal written
advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
Dear Mr. Schneiderman:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of mernbers of the Mi-
Wuk/Sugar Pine Fire Protection District for advice on provisions of the political Reform
Act (the "Act').1 We do not offer advice on past conduct; this letterrefers only to actions
that have not yet taken place. (Regulatiol 1g329(bxgXa).),Because yo* qrrotions are
general in nafure, we provide you with informal assistance.2
QUESTIONS
1. May the mernbers of the district's board vote on a policy gnder which the
district will purchase supplies from local businesses?
2. May the board members establish a policy pennitting volunteer firefighters to
post business cards, advertisements, and unrrouoceroents of local interest on
the fire station's bulletin board, which is used by district personnel and
members ofthe local communit5fl


File No. I-04-228
Page No. 2
2. Yes. The Act does not prohibit the board from establishing a policy
regarding use of this bulletin board.
FACTS
The Mi-Wul/Sugar Pine Fire Protection Dishict is located about l6 miles east of
Sonora, California along the State Highway 108 corridor. The dishict includes roughly
one square mile of mixed residential and commercial properties, including restaurants, a
gas/mini market and auto parts/rqak facility, a village market, real estatJoffices, etc.
Immediately adjacent to the district is another community that is smaller but provides
some similar services. The firehouse contains a bulletin board which is used-by district
personnel and others to post notices oflocal interest, which can be viewod by district
personnel and visitors to the firehouse.
The district has five memb€rs on its board of directors, a full-time fire chief and
assistant fire chie{, a part-time secretary, and l6 to 20 volunteer fire fighters. The board
members, the fire chief, and the assistant fire chief file statements of economic interests
(SEIs) under the district's conflict of interest code. The secretary and the volunteer
firefighters do not file SEIs. None oftheboard memb€rs has an economic interest in the
local businesses from which the diskict might purchase groceries or similar supplies; the
fire chief does or may have an economic interest in the rillag" market. fhe board may
consider establishing a policy that would govern regular and-routine purchases by district
personnel of supplies for the firehouse.
ANALYSIS
Question 1:
A decision on the expenditure of district funds to purchase supplies implicates the
Act's conflict of interest rules, which generallyprohibit puUtic officials from making
participating in making or using their official positions io influence the making of a=
governmental decision in which theyhave a financial interest. The general rutI, Urieny
stated, is that a conflict of interest exists when a public official plays a decision-makin;
role in a govemmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeablq material
financial effect on one ormore of the official's economic interests. The Commission uses
an eight-step analytical framework to determine whether apublic official has a
disqualiffing conflicl of interest (Regulation 1S700(bXl)--(S), copy enclosed) which we
outline to illustrate the analysis of a decision by the board r"tdrg u poti"y for district
purchases in light ofthe circumstances you describe.
Step 1: Are the board members public olJicials?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to ..public officials."
(Sections87l00,87ll3;regulationl870l.) "PublicOfficial-isdefinedbytheActto
include every "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
ageficy," with some exceptions not relevant here. (Section 82048.) "Localgovemment


File No. I-04-228
Page No. 3
agency means a county, city or district of any kind including a school diskict, or any
9fto local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau, offr"",
board, commission or other agency of the foregoing." (Section 82041.) Members of the
district's board of directors are public officials forpurposes of the Act.
Step Two: Will the board members be making, participating in moking, or using their
ofricial positions to influence a governmental decisioi?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions aryly only wherea public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a govemmental decision in whichhi [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [or she] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; iegulation lgTOO(b)e).) The
Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define'?naking,,.lardcipating in
making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, which provide i roio*r,
o A public official 'lnakes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within
the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 1g702.1.)
o A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or in-iernening
review, the official negotiates, advises ormakes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmentar decision. (Regulation I g702.2.)
o A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision i{, for the purpose of influe,ncing the official contacts or appears before any
mernber, officer, ernployee, or consultant ofhis orher agency. (Regulation ttlOZ.i.13
If a public official does not make, participate in making or gse his or her official
position to influence a govemmental decision, there can be no conflict of inte,rest as
defined by the Act. Your question presupposes that the board members would be making
or participating in making a govemmental decision when they establish a policy for the
purchase of district supplies. A disqualifring conflict ofinterest is therefoie possible.
step Three: Economic interests, the possibre sources of a conflia of interesr
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest'in a
govemmental decision 'tf it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on thepublic generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of tfr" ofucial's-economic
interests, described as follows:
3 Regulation 18702-4lists a series of exceptions to the rules stated in the preceding tlree regulations, which
apply only in certain limited circumstances.


File No. I-04-229
Page No. 4
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect invesknent of $2,000 or more (section Slt}Z(a1;regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, tnrstee, employee, or
holds anyposition of managernent (section S7103(d); regulation 18703.1@)); 
-
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section SZf Of 6y; regulation 1g703.2);
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomq including
promised incomq which aggregates to $500 ormore within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 1 03 (c); regulation I g703.3);
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to th" d""iriot (section
871 03(e); regulation t870B.g;
o A public official always has an economic interest in his orherpersonal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family- this is the'personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation Ig703.5).
You have not described the economic interests of the board members, but indicate
that none of them has any economic interest in any individual orbusiness from which the
district might procure the supplies in question. Assuming further that this decision would
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial "fe"t on any of the board members,
personal finances, it does not appear that any of the bomd members has an economic
interest that could be affected by a governmental decision setting district procgrement
policies.a The conflicts analysis tlJrefore ends at this point, with a conclusion that the
board members do not have a conflict of interest in a polirydecision setting protocols for
the routine purchase of supplies from local vendors.s
We close by noting that laws outside the Act, such as Government Code section
1090, may also be implicated whenever a govemment age,ncy e,nters into a contract with
an agency official, or with any business in which the official has an economic interest
We cannot provideadvige on laws beyond our jurisdiction, but we can advise you to
confer with the Office of the Attorney General about possible applications of
n of course, if the board were to adopt a policy that simply confenred discretion in procurement decisions
on a public official who had an economic interest in ore of the potential suppliers,it Ir pooiui" that this
official would have a conflict of interest in exercising the decision.making authority deiegatJ by the
board. For present purposes, we assume that any sucl di".r"tionary r.rtnJrity o,orrl-a be d"f"!rt"O to u
person who is not a public official, or to a public official who did not have an economic interest in any of
the potential vendors. Our analysis is therefore limited to potential conflicts of interest involving the board
members.
] rte next (fourth) step in the analytical process determines whether the ofEcials' established economic
interests are directly or indirectly involved in the decision; the fifth and sixth steps keat th foreseeability
ofa material financial effect oa t}te officials' interests. The last two steps presume a finding ofreasonably
foreseeable, material financial effect on at least one economic interest, and determine whether the official
may nonetheless vote, under certain circumstances.


FileNo. t-04-228
Page No. 5
Government Code section 1090, if the district conternplates entering a conkact with any
business owned in whole or in part by one of the district,s public offi"iulr.
Question 2:
As public officials acting in their official capacities, the board members would be
making a governmental decision if they were to estiblish a policy regarding use of the
firehouse bulletin board. You have rroi, ho*"rer, suggested that such a decision would
have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the economic interest of any
board member- So long as that is the case, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would
not bar anyboard member from voting on such apolicy. Noris there anything in the Act
that would prohibit the district's volunteer firefighters from posting theiibusiiess cards
or similar commercial notices, or announcements of upcoming "rit* that may be of
interest to dishict personnel or visiting members of the public, on this bulletin board.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General CounselLTM
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, I*gal Division
Enclosures
LTrit/jg

QUESTION: 1. May the mernbers of the district's board vote on a policy gnder which the
district will purchase supplies from local businesses?
2. May the board members establish a policy pennitting volunteer firefighters to
post business cards, advertisements, and unrrouoceroents of local interest on
the fire station's bulletin board, which is used by district personnel and
members ofthe local communit5fl
2. Yes. The Act does not prohibit the board from establishing a policy
regarding use of this bulletin board.

CONCLUSION: I ' Yes. As we understand the facts, the board members do not have a conflict
of interest under the Act in voting on a policy under which the district will
purchase supplies from local businesses.
' Go'ernment Code sections 81000 - gl)l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections lgl09-
18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not confei the immunityprovided by a Commission opinion or formal written
advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
